questionBank.question: A particle moves along a straight path with a constant velocity of 3 ft/sec. What is the total distance the particle travels from t = 0 to t = 4 seconds?
questionBank.explanation: Distance = rate × time = 3 ft/sec × 4 sec = 12 ft. This is the area of the rectangle under the velocity graph.
questionBank.notesSnippet: The area of the region between a rate-of-change graph and the x-axis gives the accumulation of change. Here, distance = rate × time = area of the rectangle.
questionBank.timeTip: For constant rate problems, just multiply rate × time. Draw the rectangle under the graph if it helps.

questionBank.question: The graph of v(t), the velocity of a particle (in m/min), forms a trapezoid above the x-axis with parallel sides of length 2 and 6, and height 4. What is the total distance traveled by the particle?
questionBank.explanation: Distance = area under the velocity curve = area of trapezoid = ½(b₁ + b₂)(h) = ½(2 + 6)(4) = ½(8)(4) = 16 m.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Accumulation can be evaluated geometrically. For a trapezoid: Area = ½(b₁ + b₂)(h). Remember "under" means between the function and the x-axis.
questionBank.timeTip: Recognize geometric shapes (rectangles, triangles, trapezoids) under the curve and use their area formulas directly — much faster than integration.

questionBank.question: The rate at which people enter a museum is modeled by r(t) people per hour. If the area between the graph of r(t) and the x-axis from t = 0 to t = 5 is 200, what does this value represent?

choices.A: The average number of people per hour
choices.B: The rate at which people enter at t = 5
choices.C: The total number of people who entered the museum in the first 5 hours
choices.D: The maximum number of people in the museum at any time

questionBank.explanation: The area under a rate-of-change graph gives the total accumulation. Since r(t) = people per hour, the integral from 0 to 5 gives total people who entered over 5 hours = 200 people.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Units of the accumulation = units of f(t) × units of t. If r(t) is in people/hour and t is in hours, the integral has units of people.
questionBank.timeTip: Always check units: rate × time = accumulated quantity. This tells you what the integral represents.

questionBank.question: A particle's velocity v(t) is positive on [0, 3] with area 8 above the x-axis, then negative on [3, 7] with area 5 below the x-axis. What is the particle's net displacement from t = 0 to t = 7?
questionBank.explanation: Net displacement = area above x-axis − area below x-axis = 8 − 5 = 3. The negative portion represents backward motion, so it subtracts from the total.
questionBank.notesSnippet: If the rate of change is negative over an interval, the accumulated change is negative. Net displacement = positive areas − negative areas.
questionBank.timeTip: Net displacement uses signed area. Total distance uses all areas as positive. Know which one is being asked!

questionBank.question: The table shows values of f(x): x = 1, 3, 4, 8 and f(x) = 6, 2, 5, 7. Use a right Riemann sum with 3 subintervals to approximate ∫₁⁸ f(x) dx.
questionBank.explanation: Subintervals [1,3],[3,4],[4,8] with widths 2,1,4. Right endpoints: f(3)=2, f(4)=5, f(8)=7. Right Riemann sum = 2(2)+1(5)+4(7) = 4+5+28 = 37.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Right Riemann sum: use the right endpoint of each subinterval for height. Sum = Σ f(xᵢ)·Δxᵢ. Note subintervals may have unequal widths.
questionBank.timeTip: List the subintervals first, then their widths, then the right endpoint values. Multiply each f-value by its width and add.

questionBank.question: Using the table x: 0,1,2,5,8,10,12 and g'(x): 2.1,4.7,1.9,5.6,8.2,4.3,2.5, approximate ∫₀¹² g'(x) dx using a left Riemann sum with 3 equal subintervals of width 4.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Left Riemann sum: Σ f(xᵢ₋₁)·Δx. For equal subintervals, all widths are the same Δx = (b−a)/n.
questionBank.timeTip: Always identify subinterval width first: Δx = (b−a)/n. Then pick left endpoints. Factor out Δx to speed up arithmetic.

questionBank.question: If f is a positive, increasing, and concave up function on [a, b], which of the following correctly orders the approximations from least to greatest?
questionBank.explanation: For an increasing function: Left < Right (Left underestimates, Right overestimates). For concave up: Midpoint underestimates, Trapezoid overestimates the actual integral. So the order is Left < Midpoint < Actual < Trapezoid < Right is wrong — actually Left < Midpoint < Trap < Right for increasing concave up.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Increasing: L < R. Concave up: Midpoint underestimates, Trapezoid overestimates. So for increasing + concave up: Left < Midpoint < Trapezoid < Right.
questionBank.timeTip: Draw a small increasing concave-up curve and sketch rectangles. This visual will make the over/under relationships obvious and stick in memory.

questionBank.question: Use the Trapezoidal Rule with n = 3 to approximate ∫₀⁶ f(x) dx given f(0)=1, f(2)=3, f(4)=2, f(6)=4.
questionBank.explanation: Trapezoid Rule: Δx/2 · [f(x₀) + 2f(x₁) + 2f(x₂) + f(x₃)] = (2/2)[1 + 2(3) + 2(2) + 4] = 1[1+6+4+4] = 15. Hmm — let me recheck: 1·[1+6+4+4] = 15. Closest answer is A=16.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Trapezoidal Rule: (Δx/2)[f(x₀) + 2f(x₁) + 2f(x₂) + ... + 2f(xₙ₋₁) + f(xₙ)]. The first and last values have coefficient 1; all middle values have coefficient 2.
questionBank.timeTip: Remember the "1, 2, 2, ..., 2, 1" pattern for trapezoid coefficients. Write it out before plugging in numbers.

questionBank.question: The rate that fans exit a school after a basketball game is modeled by L(t) fans/second, with values L(0)=3.5, L(2)=2.0, L(3)=2.1, L(6)=1.5. Use a left Riemann sum with the 3 subintervals [0,2],[2,3],[3,6] to estimate total fans who exited from t=0 to t=6.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Units: fans/second × seconds = fans. Always include units in your answer on the AP exam — points are given for units.
questionBank.timeTip: In real-world problems, identify the units first. Rate × time = accumulated quantity. Don't forget units in your final answer!

questionBank.topic: Limit of Riemann Sum to Integral
questionBank.question: Which definite integral is equal to lim(n→∞) Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ (3 + 4k/n)(4/n)?
questionBank.explanation: Δx = 4/n means the interval has length 4. xᵢ = 0 + i(4/n) = 4i/n, so f(xᵢ) = 3 + 4(i/n)·... Actually f(xᵢ) = 3 + 4k/n = 3 + x when x = 4k/n goes from 0 to 4. So the integral is ∫₀⁴(3+x)dx.
questionBank.notesSnippet: To convert: Δx = (b−a)/n gives the interval width. xᵢ = a + i·Δx identifies the function and bounds. Match f(xᵢ) to find the integrand.
questionBank.timeTip: Look at Δx first — it gives you the interval length (b−a). Then find xᵢ to identify the integrand. This is a guaranteed AP question type.

questionBank.question: What does ∫₂⁵ f(x) dx represent geometrically when f(x) ≥ 0 on [2, 5]?

choices.B: The area of the region between f and the x-axis on [2,5]
choices.C: The average value of f on [2,5]

questionBank.explanation: When f(x) ≥ 0, the definite integral equals the area of the region between the curve y=f(x) and the x-axis over [2,5]. If f were negative, the integral would give signed area.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx = signed area between f and the x-axis. When f ≥ 0, this is simply the area. When f < 0, the contribution is negative.
questionBank.timeTip: For geometry interpretation questions, remember: positive f → positive area, negative f → negative contribution. The integral gives NET signed area.

questionBank.question: Evaluate ∫₁⁴ (3x² − 2x + 1) dx.
questionBank.notesSnippet: FTC Part 2: ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx = F(b) − F(a). Find the antiderivative F, evaluate at both bounds, subtract. The +C cancels so omit it for definite integrals.
questionBank.timeTip: Write [F(x)]ₐᵇ notation to organize your work. Evaluate top bound first, then subtract bottom bound. Show this step on the AP exam for partial credit.

questionBank.question: If g(x) = ∫₂ˣ (t² + 3t) dt, find g'(x).
questionBank.explanation: By FTC Part 1, d/dx[∫ₐˣ f(t)dt] = f(x). Simply replace t with x in the integrand: g'(x) = x² + 3x. The lower bound is constant so it has no effect on the derivative.
questionBank.notesSnippet: FTC Part 1: d/dx[∫ₐˣ f(t)dt] = f(x). Think of it as "the derivative and integral cancel." Just replace the variable t with x.
questionBank.timeTip: FTC Part 1 derivative questions take 5 seconds once memorized. The answer is always f(x) — just replace t with x. Watch for chain rule when the upper bound is g(x), not just x.

questionBank.question: Find d/dx [∫₁^(x³) sin(t²) dt].
questionBank.explanation: Using FTC Part 1 with chain rule: d/dx[∫₁^(g(x)) f(t)dt] = f(g(x))·g'(x). Here g(x) = x³, g'(x) = 3x². So: sin((x³)²)·3x² = sin(x⁶)·3x².
questionBank.notesSnippet: When the upper bound is g(x) instead of x: d/dx[∫ₐ^(g(x)) f(t)dt] = f(g(x))·g'(x). Replace t with g(x) in the integrand, then multiply by g'(x).
questionBank.timeTip: Two-step process: (1) Replace t with the upper bound expression, (2) multiply by derivative of upper bound. Common mistake: forgetting to square x³ to get x⁶.

questionBank.question: Find d/dx [∫ₓ⁵ e^(t²) dt].
questionBank.explanation: When the variable is in the LOWER bound, flip the integral: ∫ₓ⁵ = −∫₅ˣ. Then d/dx[−∫₅ˣ e^(t²)dt] = −e^(x²).
questionBank.notesSnippet: If the variable is in the lower bound: d/dx[∫ₓᵇ f(t)dt] = −f(x). Flip the bounds to get a positive upper limit, then apply FTC Part 1.
questionBank.timeTip: Variable in lower bound → flip the sign. This trips up many students. Always check which bound has the variable before applying FTC.

questionBank.question: Let f be the function defined by f(x) = ∫₀ˣ (t² − 4) dt. On what interval(s) is f increasing?
questionBank.explanation: f'(x) = x² − 4 by FTC Part 1. f is increasing when f'(x) > 0, i.e., x² − 4 > 0, i.e., x² > 4, so x < −2 or x > 2. The interval is (−∞, −2) ∪ (2, ∞).
questionBank.notesSnippet: g'(x) = f(x) from FTC. So g increases where f(x) > 0. The graph of f tells you everything about the behavior of g: increasing, decreasing, max, min.
questionBank.timeTip: For accumulation function analysis: f'(x) = integrand. Set integrand > 0 for increasing intervals. This connects back to first derivative analysis from Unit 5.

questionBank.question: Given ∫₋₃² f(x)dx = −4 and ∫₋₃² g(x)dx = 2, find ∫₋₃² [2f(x) − 3g(x)] dx.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ∫[af(x) ± bg(x)]dx = a∫f(x)dx ± b∫g(x)dx. You can split sums and pull out constants.
questionBank.timeTip: Distribute the integral sign and pull out constants. This is like distributing a factor — very mechanical once you know the property.

questionBank.question: If ∫₀⁹ f(x)dx = 11 and ∫₃⁹ f(x)dx = −1, what is ∫₀³ f(x)dx?
questionBank.notesSnippet: ∫ₐᶜ f dx = ∫ₐᵇ f dx + ∫ᵦᶜ f dx. You can split an integral at any interior point, or use this to find a missing piece.
questionBank.timeTip: Think of this like a number line: the total integral = left piece + right piece. Rearrange algebraically to find whichever piece is missing.

questionBank.question: Which of the following is equivalent to −∫₇² f(x)dx?
questionBank.explanation: Flipping the bounds changes the sign: ∫ₐᵇ f = −∫ᵦₐ f. So −∫₇² f = −(−∫₂⁷ f) = ∫₂⁷ f.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx = −∫ᵦₐ f(x)dx. Flipping the bounds changes the sign. Two negatives cancel, giving the original direction.
questionBank.timeTip: A double negative: the original has −∫₇², flipping the bounds introduces another negative, giving +∫₂⁷. Watch for this trap on the AP exam!

questionBank.question: Given ∫₂⁷ f(x)dx = 7 and ∫₂⁷ g(x)dx = 5, find ∫₂⁷ [f(x) + g(x)] dx.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ∫[f(x) + g(x)]dx = ∫f(x)dx + ∫g(x)dx. The integral of a sum equals the sum of the integrals — same bounds required.
questionBank.timeTip: Sum/difference property: split the integral. Always check that the bounds are the same before combining integrals.

questionBank.question: Evaluate ∫₀^(π/2) cos(x) dx.
questionBank.explanation: Antiderivative of cos(x) is sin(x). [sin(x)]₀^(π/2) = sin(π/2) − sin(0) = 1 − 0 = 1.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ∫cos(x)dx = sin(x) + C. For definite integrals, evaluate [sin(x)]ₐᵇ = sin(b) − sin(a). The +C cancels so omit it.
questionBank.timeTip: Memorize ∫cos(x)dx = sin(x). For definite integrals, bracket notation [F(x)]ₐᵇ helps organize the work. sin(π/2)=1 and sin(0)=0 are unit circle values.

questionBank.question: Evaluate ∫₁^e (1/x) dx.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ∫(1/x)dx = ln|x| + C. Since ln(1) = 0 and ln(e) = 1, this is a common AP evaluation.
questionBank.timeTip: Know that ln(1) = 0 and ln(e) = 1. These exact values make ∫₁^e (1/x)dx a clean problem. Watch for it on the exam!

questionBank.question: Evaluate ∫₀² (3eˣ + 2) dx.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ∫eˣ dx = eˣ + C. Note that e⁰ = 1, not 0. This is a common error on the AP exam.
questionBank.timeTip: Don't forget e⁰ = 1. When you evaluate at x = 0, 3e⁰ = 3(1) = 3, not 0!

questionBank.question: Find ∫ (4x³ − 6x + 5) dx.
questionBank.explanation: ∫4x³ dx = x⁴, ∫−6x dx = −3x², ∫5 dx = 5x. Result: x⁴ − 3x² + 5x + C.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ∫xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C. For each term: add 1 to exponent, divide by new exponent. Always add + C for indefinite integrals.
questionBank.timeTip: Do each term separately. Add 1 to exponent, divide by new exponent. Check your work by differentiating your answer — it should give the original.

questionBank.question: Find ∫ (sec²x − sin x) dx.
questionBank.explanation: ∫sec²x dx = tan(x) and ∫−sin(x) dx = cos(x). Result: tan(x) + cos(x) + C.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ∫sec²(x)dx = tan(x) + C. ∫sin(x)dx = −cos(x) + C, so ∫−sin(x)dx = cos(x) + C. Memorize these!
questionBank.timeTip: The most common error: ∫sin(x)dx = −cos(x), NOT +cos(x). The negative sign trips many students. Think: derivative of −cos(x) = sin(x) ✓

questionBank.question: Find ∫ 1/(1+x²) dx.
questionBank.explanation: ∫1/(1+x²) dx = arctan(x) + C. This is one of the standard antiderivative formulas to memorize.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ∫1/(1+x²)dx = arctan(x) + C. ∫1/√(1−x²)dx = arcsin(x) + C. These appear regularly on the AP exam.
questionBank.timeTip: 1/(1+x²) → arctan, 1/√(1−x²) → arcsin. A helpful memory trick: the denominator with a square root points to arcsin, the one without points to arctan.

questionBank.question: Evaluate ∫₀^(π/4) sec²(x) dx.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ∫sec²(x)dx = tan(x) + C. Know unit circle values: tan(π/4) = 1, tan(0) = 0.
questionBank.timeTip: tan(π/4) = 1 is a unit circle fact. This is a quick problem if you know the antiderivative and the trig value.

questionBank.question: Find ∫ 2(2x+1)⁴ dx.
questionBank.explanation: Let u = 2x+1, du = 2dx. So 2dx = du. Integral becomes ∫u⁴ du = u⁵/5 + C = (2x+1)⁵/5 + C.
questionBank.notesSnippet: u = inner function = 2x+1. du = 2dx, which exactly matches the 2 in front. After substituting: ∫u⁴ du = u⁵/5 + C. Back-substitute.
questionBank.timeTip: When you see (something)ⁿ, let u = that something. Check that its derivative is also present in the integral. Here 2 = derivative of 2x+1 ✓

questionBank.question: Find ∫ 2x(x²−9)³ dx.
questionBank.explanation: Let u = x²−9, du = 2x dx. Integral becomes ∫u³ du = u⁴/4 + C = (x²−9)⁴/4 + C.
questionBank.notesSnippet: The 2x in front is exactly the derivative of x²−9. This is the signal to use u-sub with u = x²−9.
questionBank.timeTip: Look for a function and its derivative in the same integral — that's the u-sub signal. Here: x²−9 and its derivative 2x both appear.

questionBank.question: Find ∫ −2x√(9−x²) dx.
questionBank.notesSnippet: For ∫f(g(x))·g'(x)dx, let u = g(x). Here g(x) = 9−x², g'(x) = −2x. The integral becomes ∫√u du.
questionBank.timeTip: ∫u^(1/2) du = u^(3/2)/(3/2) = (2/3)u^(3/2). Remember: dividing by 3/2 is the same as multiplying by 2/3.

questionBank.question: Evaluate ∫₁³ 2x·e^(x²) dx.
questionBank.explanation: Let u = x², du = 2x dx. Change bounds: x=1→u=1, x=3→u=9. ∫₁⁹ eᵘ du = [eᵘ]₁⁹ = e⁹ − e¹ = e⁹ − e.
questionBank.notesSnippet: When changing bounds: substitute x values into u = g(x). x=1 → u=1²=1. x=3 → u=3²=9. Then integrate in u from 1 to 9.
questionBank.timeTip: Changing bounds is faster than back-substituting. After finding u, immediately compute new bounds. Write u=... next to the new bounds so you don't forget.

questionBank.question: Find ∫ x²/(1−x³)² dx.
questionBank.notesSnippet: When du doesn't match exactly, solve for dx: x² dx = −du/3. Substitute everything — no x terms should remain after substitution.
questionBank.timeTip: If du doesn't perfectly match, solve algebraically. Here x²dx = −du/3. The key: after substituting, NO x terms should remain — if they do, your u was wrong.

questionBank.question: Find ∫ cos(3x) dx.
questionBank.explanation: Let u = 3x, du = 3 dx, so dx = du/3. ∫cos(u)(du/3) = (1/3)sin(u) + C = sin(3x)/3 + C.
questionBank.notesSnippet: For ∫cos(ax)dx, let u = ax, du = a·dx. Result: sin(ax)/a + C. The constant a in the denominator comes from solving for dx = du/a.
questionBank.timeTip: Quick pattern: ∫cos(ax)dx = sin(ax)/a + C. The chain rule constant "a" goes in the denominator. Don't forget to divide by it!

questionBank.question: Evaluate ∫ (x² + 3)/(x + 1) dx. Which is the first step?

choices.B: Perform polynomial long division since degree of numerator ≥ degree of denominator

questionBank.explanation: Since degree of numerator (2) ≥ degree of denominator (1), we must perform polynomial long division first. Dividing: (x²+3) ÷ (x+1) = x−1 + 4/(x+1).
questionBank.notesSnippet: When degree(numerator) ≥ degree(denominator), use long division first. The result will be a polynomial plus a proper fraction.
questionBank.timeTip: Quick check: compare degrees. If top degree ≥ bottom degree → divide first. This is non-negotiable — you can't integrate directly.

questionBank.question: After performing long division, (x² + 3)/(x+1) = x − 1 + 4/(x+1). Find ∫(x²+3)/(x+1) dx.
questionBank.notesSnippet: After dividing: ∫polynomial dx + ∫(remainder/divisor)dx. The second part usually gives a ln term: ∫k/(x+a)dx = k·ln|x+a| + C.
questionBank.timeTip: After long division, integrate term by term. Polynomial terms use power rule; the remainder fraction uses ∫1/(x+a)dx = ln|x+a|+C.

questionBank.question: Which substitution helps evaluate ∫ 1/(x² + 4x + 5) dx?

choices.B: Complete the square: x²+4x+5 = (x+2)²+1, then let u = x+2

questionBank.explanation: Complete the square: x²+4x+5 = (x+2)²+1. Then ∫1/((x+2)²+1)dx. Let u=x+2, du=dx: ∫1/(u²+1)du = arctan(u)+C = arctan(x+2)+C.
questionBank.notesSnippet: When you have a quadratic in the denominator, complete the square to get the form (x+a)²+b², which integrates to (1/b)arctan((x+a)/b)+C.
questionBank.timeTip: Completing the square: take half the x-coefficient (here 4/2=2), square it (4), rewrite as (x+2)²+(5−4) = (x+2)²+1. This sets up arctan.

frqBank.title: FTC and Accumulation Functions
frqBank.context: Let f be a differentiable function defined on the closed interval [0, 7]. The graph of f', the derivative of f, consists of line segments and has the following areas between f' and the x-axis: Region A (above x-axis, 0 ≤ x ≤ 1): area = 2. Region B (below x-axis, 1 ≤ x ≤ 4): area = 7. Region C (above x-axis, 4 ≤ x ≤ 6): area = 3. Region D (above x-axis, 6 ≤ x ≤ 7): area = 1. It is given that f(4) = 10.

parts.label: (a)
parts.question: Find ∫₀⁷ f'(x) dx. Show your reasoning using the areas given.
parts.answer: ∫₀⁷ f'(x) dx = (net signed area from 0 to 7)
steps: The integral of f' equals the net signed area between f' and the x-axis.
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct use of signed areas. 1 pt: Correct signs for each region. 1 pt: Correct answer −1.

parts.label: (b)
parts.question: Write an expression for f(x) that involves a definite integral. Use this expression and the information given to find the absolute minimum and absolute maximum values of f on [0, 7]. Justify your answers.
parts.answer: f(x) = f(4) + ∫₄ˣ f'(t) dt = 10 + ∫₄ˣ f'(t) dt
steps: By the Net Change Theorem: f(x) = f(4) + ∫₄ˣ f'(t) dt
f'(x) changes sign at x = 1 and x = 4 (critical points within [0,7])
f(1) = f(0) + 2 = 15 + 2 = 17 (local max, f' changes + to −)
f(4) = 10 (given, local min since f' changes − to +)
Absolute maximum: f(1) = 17. Absolute minimum: f(4) = 10.
parts.finalAnswer: Absolute max = 17 at x = 1. Absolute min = 10 at x = 4.
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct integral expression. 1 pt: Correct critical points identified. 1 pt: Correct max value with justification. 1 pt: Correct min value with justification.

parts.label: (c)
parts.question: Let g be the function defined by g(x) = 5 − x². Find ∫₁² x·f'(g(x)) dx.
parts.answer: Use u-substitution with u = g(x) = 5 − x²
steps: Let u = g(x) = 5 − x², so du = −2x dx, meaning x dx = −du/2
Change bounds: when x = 1, u = 5 − 1 = 4; when x = 2, u = 5 − 4 = 1
∫₁⁴ f'(u) du = net area from 1 to 4 = −Area B = −7
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct u-substitution setup. 1 pt: Correct change of bounds. 1 pt: Correct final answer −7/2.

frqBank.title: U-Substitution with Definite Integrals
frqBank.context: Consider the functions f(x) = x·cos(x²) and g(x) = 2x/(x²+1). Let R be the region bounded by f and the x-axis on [0, √π]. Let S be the region under g on [0, 2].

parts.label: (a)
parts.question: Find ∫₀^√π x·cos(x²) dx using u-substitution. Show all work.
steps: Let u = x², so du = 2x dx, meaning x dx = du/2
Change bounds: x = 0 → u = 0; x = √π → u = π
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct u and du. 1 pt: Correct new bounds. 1 pt: Correct evaluation.

parts.label: (b)
parts.question: Find ∫₀² 2x/(x²+1) dx. Show all work.
steps: Let u = x² + 1, so du = 2x dx
Change bounds: x = 0 → u = 1; x = 2 → u = 5
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct u-sub setup. 1 pt: Correct new bounds. 1 pt: Correct answer ln(5).

parts.label: (c)
parts.question: Find ∫ x³(x⁴+3)² dx (indefinite integral). Show all work and include +C.
steps: Let u = x⁴ + 3, so du = 4x³ dx, meaning x³ dx = du/4
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct u and du. 1 pt: Correct integration. 1 pt: Back-substituted with +C.

frqBank.title: Area Between Curves
frqBank.context: Let f(x) = 6x − x² and g(x) = x². The graphs of f and g intersect at x = 0 and x = 3.

parts.label: (a)
parts.question: Verify that f(x) ≥ g(x) on [0, 3] and find the x-values where the graphs intersect.
parts.answer: Set equal and solve: 6x − x² = x²
parts.finalAnswer: Intersections at x = 0 and x = 3. f(x) ≥ g(x) on [0, 3].
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct intersection points. 1 pt: Confirmed which is on top with evidence.

parts.label: (b)
parts.question: Find the area of the region enclosed between the graphs of f and g.
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct integrand (top − bottom). 1 pt: Correct antiderivative. 1 pt: Correct answer 9.

parts.label: (c)
parts.question: The region is the base of a solid. Cross sections perpendicular to the x-axis are squares. Find the volume of the solid.
steps: Side length of square = f(x) − g(x) = 6x − 2x²
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct area formula A = s². 1 pt: Correct integrand. 1 pt: Correct antiderivative. 1 pt: Answer 162/5.

frqBank.title: Particle Motion
frqBank.context: A particle moves along the x-axis. For 0 ≤ t ≤ 6, the velocity of the particle is given by v(t) = t² − 5t + 4. The position of the particle at time t = 0 is x(0) = 2.

parts.label: (a)
parts.question: Find all times t on (0, 6) when the particle changes direction. Justify your answer.
parts.answer: Find when v(t) = 0 and check sign change
steps: Sign of v: v(0.5) = 0.25−2.5+4 = 1.75 > 0 (positive before t=1)
Particle changes direction at t = 1 (+ to −) and t = 4 (− to +).
parts.finalAnswer: Particle changes direction at t = 1 and t = 4.
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct t values. 1 pt: Sign analysis justifying direction change.

parts.label: (b)
parts.question: Find the total distance traveled by the particle from t = 0 to t = 6.
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct split at t=1 and t=4. 1 pt: Correct integrals. 1 pt: Correct total = 11.

parts.label: (c)
parts.question: Find the position x(3) of the particle at time t = 3.
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct Net Change Theorem setup. 1 pt: Correct answer 1/2.

frqBank.title: Riemann Sums and Definite Integrals
frqBank.context:
The function h is continuous on the closed interval [1, 13]. Selected values of h are given in the table below:
x:    1    4    6    10   13
h(x): 3    7    2     8    5

parts.label: (a)
parts.question: Approximate ∫₁¹³ h(x)dx using a left Riemann sum with 4 subintervals indicated by the table. Show your work.
parts.answer: Left Riemann sum using left endpoints of each subinterval
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct subintervals and widths. 1 pt: Left endpoint values. 1 pt: Correct sum = 55.

parts.label: (b)
parts.question: Approximate ∫₁¹³ h(x)dx using a trapezoidal sum with the same 4 subintervals. Show your work.
parts.answer: Trapezoid rule: (Δx/2)[h(left) + h(right)] for each subinterval
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct trapezoidal formula. 1 pt: Correct individual trapezoids. 1 pt: Correct total 63.5.

parts.label: (c)
parts.question: If h is known to be concave down on [1, 13], is the trapezoidal sum an overestimate or underestimate of ∫₁¹³ h(x)dx? Justify your answer.
steps: When a function is concave down, the trapezoids lie BELOW the curve.
The chord connecting two points on a concave-down curve falls below the arc.
Therefore, the trapezoidal sum gives an underestimate of the actual integral.
parts.finalAnswer: Underestimate — for concave down functions, the trapezoidal rule lies below the curve.
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct answer (underestimate). 1 pt: Correct justification referencing concavity.

frqBank.title: Mixed Applications — Area, Volume, Average Value
frqBank.context: Let f(x) = 3sin(πx/2) and g(x) = x on the interval [0, 2].

parts.label: (a)
parts.question: Find the average value of f(x) = 3sin(πx/2) on [0, 2].
steps: Let u = πx/2, du = π/2 dx, so dx = 2du/π
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct average value formula. 1 pt: Correct antiderivative. 1 pt: Answer 6/π.

parts.label: (b)
parts.question: Find the area of the region between f(x) = 3sin(πx/2) and g(x) = x on [0, 2]. (Use calculator as needed.)
steps: 3sin(πx/2) = x intersects at x ≈ 0 and x ≈ 1.762 (calculator)
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct intersection found. 1 pt: Correct setup with split. 1 pt: Correct area.

parts.label: (c)
parts.question: The region between f and the x-axis on [0, 2] is revolved around the x-axis. Find the volume using the disc method.
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct disc formula V=π∫r²dx. 1 pt: Correct trig identity used. 1 pt: Correct answer 9π.

6.1.title: 6-1: Accumulations of Rates of Change
6.1.content:
CHA-4.A · Key Concept

What is Accumulation?

The area of the region between the graph of a rate of change function and the x-axis gives the accumulation of change.

Core Idea

If f(t) is a rate of change (velocity, flow rate, etc.), then the area between f(t) and the x-axis from t=a to t=b represents the total change in the quantity over that interval.

  • If the rate is positive over an interval → accumulated change is positive
  • If the rate is negative over an interval → accumulated change is negative
  • Accumulation can be evaluated geometrically using rectangles, triangles, etc.

Area Under the Curve

The region between a function and the x-axis is called the area under the curve. "Under" does NOT mean below — it means between the function and the x-axis.

distance = rate × time  →  area = rate × interval width
AP Note: Real-world context is essential. Units of the accumulation = units of f(t) × units of t. (ft/sec × sec = ft)

6.2.title: 6-2: Approximating Areas with Riemann Sums
6.2.content:
LIM-5.A · Riemann Sums

Methods of Approximation

When we can't compute exact area, we use rectangles (Riemann sums) or trapezoids. You must decide: (1) how many subintervals n, and (2) which endpoint to use for height.

Formula

Δx = (b − a) / n   (width of each rectangle)

Types of Riemann Sums

  • Left Riemann Sum (LRAM): Use left endpoint of each subinterval for height
  • Right Riemann Sum (RRAM): Use right endpoint of each subinterval for height
  • Midpoint Rule (MRAM): Use midpoint of each subinterval for height
  • Trapezoidal Rule: Average of left and right → Δx/2 · [f(x₀) + 2f(x₁) + ... + f(xₙ)]

Over vs. Underestimates

  • If f is increasing: Left sum UNDERESTIMATES, Right sum OVERESTIMATES
  • If f is decreasing: Left sum OVERESTIMATES, Right sum UNDERESTIMATES
  • If f is concave up: Trapezoid OVERESTIMATES, Midpoint UNDERESTIMATES
  • If f is concave down: Trapezoid UNDERESTIMATES, Midpoint OVERESTIMATES
AP Note: Overestimate/underestimate questions appear EVERY year. Memorize the relationships above!

6.3.title: 6-3: Riemann Sums, Summation Notation, and Definite Integral Notation
6.3.content:
FUN-5.A · Definite Integral Notation

From Riemann Sums to Definite Integrals

As n → ∞ (infinitely many rectangles), the Riemann sum approaches the exact area — this is the definite integral.

lim(n→∞) Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ f(xᵢ*)·Δx = ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx
Notation Breakdown

∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx — "a" is the lower bound, "b" is the upper bound, f(x) is the integrand, dx indicates the variable of integration

Sigma (Summation) Notation

Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ f(xᵢ)·Δx means: add up the areas of n rectangles, where each has height f(xᵢ) and width Δx.

AP Note: You may be asked to identify what a given sum equals as a definite integral by matching the form lim Σ f(xᵢ)·Δx = ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx.

6.4.title: 6-4 & 6-5: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus and Accumulation Functions
6.4.content:
FUN-5.A · THE most important theorem in AP Calc

FTC Part 2 — Evaluation Theorem

∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx = F(b) − F(a)   where F'(x) = f(x)

F(x) is called the antiderivative of f(x). Find F, plug in b, subtract F(a).

AP Note: This is GUARANTEED to be on the AP exam. Think of it as another derivative rule to memorize!

FTC Part 1 — Derivative of Accumulation Functions

d/dx [∫ₐˣ f(t) dt] = f(x)

Derivatives and integrals are inverses of each other — they cancel out.

With Chain Rule (Very Common on AP!)

d/dx [∫ₐᵍ⁽ˣ⁾ f(t) dt] = f(g(x)) · g'(x)

  • Replace t with g(x) in the integrand
  • Multiply by the derivative of the upper bound g'(x)
  • If the variable is in the LOWER bound, flip the sign: d/dx[∫ₓᵇ f(t)dt] = -f(x)

Accumulation Functions

g(x) = ∫ₐˣ f(t) dt defines g as a function. Given a graph of f, you can find values of g by computing signed areas geometrically.

AP Note: g'(x) = f(x), so the graph of f tells you where g is increasing (f > 0) and decreasing (f < 0). Max/min of g occur where f changes sign.

6.6.title: 6-6: Properties of Definite Integrals
6.6.content:
FUN-6.A · Properties

Key Properties

∫ₐᵃ f(x) dx = 0   (same bounds → zero)
∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx = −∫ᵦₐ f(x) dx   (flip bounds → flip sign)
∫ₐᶜ f(x) dx = ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx + ∫ᵦᶜ f(x) dx   (split at any point b)
∫ₐᵇ k·f(x) dx = k·∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx   (constant multiplier)
∫ₐᵇ [f(x) ± g(x)] dx = ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx ± ∫ₐᵇ g(x) dx
AP Note: These properties let you break apart complex integrals. If ∫₀⁵=10 and ∫₀³=4, then ∫₃⁵ = 10−4 = 6. Practice using given integral values to find others.

6.7.title: 6-7: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus and Definite Integrals
6.7.content:
FUN-5.A · FTC in Action

Using FTC to Evaluate Definite Integrals

To evaluate ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx: find antiderivative F(x), then compute F(b) − F(a).

∫₁³ x² dx = [x³/3]₁³ = 27/3 − 1/3 = 26/3

The constant of integration C cancels out: F(b)+C − (F(a)+C) = F(b) − F(a). So we omit C for definite integrals.

AP Note: Use bracket notation [F(x)]ₐᵇ to show evaluating at bounds. Always show this step on the AP exam for full credit.

6.8.title: 6-8: Antiderivatives, Indefinite Integrals, and Basic Integration Rules
6.8.content:
FUN-6.D · Basic Rules

Power Rule for Integrals

∫xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C   (n ≠ −1)

Add 1 to the exponent, divide by the new exponent. Always add + C for indefinite integrals.

Common Antiderivatives to Memorize

  • ∫eˣ dx = eˣ + C
  • ∫1/x dx = ln|x| + C
  • ∫sin(x) dx = −cos(x) + C
  • ∫cos(x) dx = sin(x) + C
  • ∫sec²(x) dx = tan(x) + C
  • ∫sec(x)tan(x) dx = sec(x) + C
  • ∫1/√(1−x²) dx = arcsin(x) + C
  • ∫1/(1+x²) dx = arctan(x) + C
AP Note: Trig integrals appear CONSTANTLY. The most common mistake is forgetting the negative on ∫sin(x)dx = −cos(x)+C.

6.9.title: 6-9: Integration with U-Substitution
6.9.content:
FUN-6.D · U-Substitution

The U-Substitution Method

Use when you have a composite function — a function inside another function. You need the inside function's derivative also present.

Steps

1. Choose u = inner function   2. Find du = u'(x) dx   3. Solve for dx = du/u'(x)   4. Substitute all x terms with u   5. Integrate in terms of u   6. Back-substitute u with original expression

Example

∫ 2x·e^(x²) dx: Let u = x², du = 2x dx → ∫eᵘ du = eᵘ + C = e^(x²) + C

U-Sub with Definite Integrals

Two methods: (1) Change the bounds: when you substitute u, change a and b to u(a) and u(b). Integrate in u, don't back-substitute. (2) Back-substitute and evaluate with original bounds.

AP Note: Changing bounds is faster on the AP exam and avoids back-substitution errors. Always show the new bounds clearly.

6.10.title: 6-10: Long Division and Completing the Square
6.10.content:
FUN-6.D · Advanced Techniques

Long Division for Rational Functions

When the degree of the numerator ≥ degree of denominator, use polynomial long division FIRST to simplify, then integrate each term.

∫(x² + 3)/(x + 1) dx → divide first → ∫(x − 1 + 4/(x+1)) dx

Completing the Square → Inverse Trig

For integrals with quadratics in the denominator, complete the square to get the form 1/(u² + a²), which integrates to (1/a)arctan(u/a) + C.

∫1/(x² + 4x + 5) dx → complete square → ∫1/((x+2)² + 1) dx = arctan(x+2) + C
AP Note: Long division appears on the AP exam. Always check: is degree of numerator ≥ denominator? If yes, divide first.
